summary: Sara Blakely explains how her father's dinner table tradition of celebrating failures taught her to redefine failure as not trying, rather than a negative outcome. She shares how she applies this mindset by looking for 'hidden gifts' in embarrassments and openly sharing her 'oops' moments with her team at Spanx to encourage a culture of risk-taking.

principles.statement: Failure is not trying, rather than the outcome.
principles.rationale: When you redefine failure this way, you remove the fear that stops people from taking risks, starting businesses, or creating art, which opens up life's possibilities.
principles.source_quote: failure for me became not trying versus the outcome

principles.statement: Every failure or embarrassment contains hidden gifts.
principles.rationale: By actively looking for and writing down what you gained from a bad situation, you realize there is always an amazing nugget of learning you wouldn't want to pass up.
principles.source_quote: write down where where the hidden gifts were and what I got out of it and I started realizing in everything there was some amazing nugget

principles.statement: Leaders should openly share their failures to encourage their team.
principles.rationale: Openly talking about your own mistakes normalizes failure and encourages the team to take risks without fear of retribution.
principles.source_quote: to encourage people to fail I'm bringing up my failures in front of the team often

frameworks.name: Finding the Hidden Gifts
frameworks.when_to_apply: Anytime something doesn't go the way you expected it to, or you get embarrassed by a situation.
steps: Acknowledge the unexpected outcome or embarrassing situation.
Write down where the hidden gifts were in that experience.
Identify exactly what you got out of it to find the 'amazing nugget'.
frameworks.source_quote: anytime something didn't go the way I expected it to or maybe I got embarrassed by a situation to write down where where the hidden gifts were and what I got out of it

mental_models.name: Redefining Failure
mental_models.description: Viewing failure not as a negative outcome or a lack of success, but simply as the act of not trying at all.
mental_models.example: Trying out for a play and being horrible at it is considered a success worth celebrating, because the attempt itself is the victory.

mental_models.name: The Oops of Spanx
mental_models.description: A cultural lens where mistakes are branded as an 'oops' rather than a catastrophic error, making them approachable, shareable, and educational.
mental_models.example: Standing up in front of the company to share a recent mistake you made to show the team that failing is an acceptable and expected part of the job.

heuristics: If you learn from it and if you can laugh about it, then it's all worth it.

signature_quotes.text: failure for me became not trying versus the outcome
signature_quotes.context: Explaining how her father's dinner table tradition changed her mindset.

signature_quotes.text: once you redefine that for yourself and realize the failure is just not trying then life opens up to you in many ways
signature_quotes.context: Discussing the benefits of overcoming the fear of failure.

signature_quotes.text: if there's a failure quote or an oops in your life if you learn from it and if you can laugh about it then it's all worth it
signature_quotes.context: Summarizing her philosophy on making mistakes.

anti_patterns.description: Not taking risks, starting a business, or creating art because of the fear of a negative outcome.
anti_patterns.why_it_fails: Because defining failure by the outcome rather than the effort keeps you paralyzed and prevents life from opening up to you.
